specialism.name: docs-readme
specialism.task: Write or improve the project-root README.md. The README is the GitHub landing page: its primary job is to pitch the package in one glance, provide a minimal runnable demo, and route interested readers to the full documentation. It must render correctly on GitHub, which means no Documenter.jl syntax (@ref, @raw html, @example) — all links must be absolute URLs to the deployed docs site. The output is a README that communicates what the package does, gives a reader enough to decide if it is relevant to them, and makes it trivial to find next steps.

workflow.step: Orient yourself. Read the project's docs/src/index.md (if it exists) to understand the package's purpose and any "Quick links" navigation section that may need mirroring in the README. Note the deployed documentation URL (found in docs/make.jl, the repo's CI deploy workflow, or the existing README). This URL is the base for every doc link in the README.

Assess the current README (if one exists). Identify: (a) Missing or broken badge table. (b) Relative or @ref links that will not resolve on GitHub. (c) Whether a minimal runnable demo exists and is accurate. (d) Whether the package description answers "what does it do and why would I use it?" (e) Whether a citation or "Quick links" section is present in index.md but absent here.

Draft or edit the README following the section order below. Keep each section minimal — the README is an advertisement and a signpost, not a tutorial or API reference.
Canonical section order: 1. H1 = package name (include .jl suffix). Immediately below: one-line tagline. 2. Badge table (reference-style link definitions at bottom of file). 3. H2 "What does this package do?" — 2–4 sentences or a short bulleted list. 4. H2 "Quick start" or equivalent — copy-pasteable Julia code that installs and runs the most basic use case; include a representative output image if one exists. 5. H2 "Quick links" (if index.md has this section) — bullet list of absolute doc links. 6. H2 "Citation" (if the package has a citable publication).
Translate any @ref links from index.md to absolute URLs when mirroring a Quick links section.

Review all links and code blocks: — Every link to the documentation site must be an absolute URL. — Badge URLs must use the correct repo path and branch (check CI workflow for correct badge endpoint — do not guess badge URLs). — The code demo must be accurate: test it mentally against the current API. — Reference-style link definitions must be collected at the bottom of the file.
Verify by rendering locally if possible (e.g., a Markdown previewer or `grip`). Confirm: (a) badges render, (b) all links resolve, (c) no @ref or relative doc paths remain.

Run the docs build to confirm the overall site still builds without new warnings: julia --project=docs docs/make.jl The README itself is not processed by Documenter, but this step confirms that any pages the README links to still exist and that no earlier edits broke the docs site. Do not report the task complete if the build emits errors.

rubric.criterion: Every link to the documentation site uses a full absolute URL. No @ref, no relative paths (../../docs/...), no bare anchor fragments. GitHub cannot render Documenter.jl syntax.
The copy-paste demo installs and runs against the current public API. Package name matches the registered name in Project.toml. Output (or expected behaviour) is described.
Standard badges are present: at minimum docs (stable and/or dev) and CI status. Reference-style link definitions are used for badge image and target URLs.
A reader unfamiliar with the package can understand what it does and who it is for within the first three paragraphs. No jargon without explanation.

formatting.rule: H1 = "PackageName.jl"; tagline on the next line, plain text, no formatting.
Badge table: a Markdown table or a line of badge images; use reference-style link definitions collected at the bottom of the file. Minimum: docs badge + CI badge.
All links to the documentation site: absolute URLs (e.g., https://clima.github.io/MyPackage.jl/dev/installation/).
No @ref, no [`Symbol`](@ref), no @raw html, no @example — GitHub renders none of these.
Code blocks: triple-backtick ```julia, hand-written, copy-pasteable end-to-end.
Package description voice: third-person for what the package is ("MyPackage is a Julia library for..."); second-person imperative for the demo ("Install and run:").
Reference-style link definitions at the very bottom of the file.
formatting.example:
## Before (relative links, no badges, untagged fence)
# MyPackage
A library for solving inverse problems.
``` using MyPackage run_something() ```
[API docs](docs/src/api.md)
## After (badge table, absolute URLs, tagged fence, reference-style links)
# MyPackage.jl
A Julia library for solving inverse problems using ensemble methods.
[![Documentation][docs-img]][docs-url] [![CI Status][ci-img]][ci-url]
## What does this package do?
MyPackage implements ensemble-based inversion algorithms that estimate unknown parameters from noisy observations. It is designed for scientific computing workflows in Julia.
## Quick start
Install via the Julia package manager:
```julia using Pkg Pkg.add("MyPackage") ```
Run a minimal example:
```julia using MyPackage result = solve(MyProblem()) ```
## Quick links
- [Documentation][docs-url] - [Installation](https://clima.github.io/MyPackage.jl/dev/installation/) - [API reference](https://clima.github.io/MyPackage.jl/dev/api/)
[docs-img]: https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-dev-blue.svg [docs-url]: https://clima.github.io/MyPackage.jl/dev/ [ci-img]: https://github.com/CliMA/MyPackage.jl/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg [ci-url]: https://github.com/CliMA/MyPackage.jl/actions/workflows/ci.yml
